description: Anyone engaged in learning or study may be called a 'thunai'. The study may be self-directed, shared with peers, or guided by a teacher or mentor. 'moloi' (apprentice) is narrower: the craft is learned through practice.
articulatory_notes: Rest the tongue tip lightly against the upper front teeth for unvoiced 'th' and release into high-back rounded 'u'. Place the tongue tip against the backs of the upper front teeth for voiced dental 'n', carrying the sound through the nose into stressed central 'a'. Keep the final vowel separate and finish with high-front unrounded 'i'.
sound_symbolism: 'th' lets a narrow stream of air pass before the lips round for 'u'. The sound then settles into nasal 'n', and the final vowels separate as the tongue rises towards 'i'. The word feels attentive without becoming tight.
usage_notes: 'lo thunai' is the ordinary plural. The subject studied can modify the noun or appear with the appropriate learning verb. The role applies across ages and learning arrangements; school enrolment or formal assessment may be present, but neither is required.

pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: The role leaves room for beginner's mind: attention can remain open even when the student already knows a great deal.
pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: Study has taken place through observation, recitation, conversation, and solitary practice. It also grows from work beside more experienced people.

semantic_domains.activity: taking part in study or learning
semantic_domains.cognition: directed attention towards acquiring understanding or skill
semantic_domains.community: a social role that may be recognised inside or outside an institution
